question: A 58-year-old male presents at a local hardware store clutching his chest. He is diaphoretic and appears anxious. He tells you the pain started about 40 minutes ago while he was loading bags of concrete into his truck. He describes the pain as a heavy pressure in the center of his chest radiating to his left arm and jaw rated 9 out of 10. He has a history of hypertension high cholesterol and smokes one pack of cigarettes daily. His medications include lisinopril hydrochlorothiazide and atorvastatin. Vital signs are BP 164/98 HR 92 RR 22 SpO2 95% on room air. You obtain a 12-lead ECG which shows ST elevation of 3mm in leads V1 through V4 with reciprocal ST depression in leads II III and aVF. Based on this presentation and ECG findings which coronary artery is most likely occluded?
option_a: Right coronary artery
option_b: Left circumflex artery
option_c: Left anterior descending artery
option_d: Posterior descending artery
rationale: ST elevation in the precordial leads V1-V4 indicates an anterior wall myocardial infarction which is caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending LAD artery. The LAD supplies the anterior wall of the left ventricle and the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum. Reciprocal changes seen as ST depression in the inferior leads II III aVF support the diagnosis of acute injury. Anterior MIs carry significant mortality risk due to the large amount of myocardium at risk.

question: You respond to a 72-year-old female at her home who called 911 for nausea and fatigue that began approximately 2 hours ago. She denies chest pain but admits to some mild shortness of breath and feeling like something is just not right. Her daughter who is present states her mother seems more confused than normal. The patient has a history of type 2 diabetes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Her medications include metformin glipizide lisinopril amlodipine and rosuvastatin. Vital signs are BP 142/88 HR 98 RR 20 SpO2 94% on room air. Blood glucose is 186 mg/dL. You obtain a 12-lead ECG which shows ST elevation of 2mm in leads II III and aVF with ST depression in leads I and aVL. What is significant about this patient's presentation?
option_a: Her symptoms are classic for inferior STEMI
option_b: She is having an atypical MI presentation common in diabetics and women where classic chest pain may be absent
option_c: Her symptoms indicate a stroke not a heart attack
option_d: She is likely having a panic attack given the vague symptoms
rationale: Women and diabetic patients frequently present with atypical symptoms of acute coronary syndrome. Instead of classic crushing chest pain they may experience fatigue weakness nausea dyspnea or vague discomfort. Diabetic neuropathy can blunt the perception of cardiac pain. This patient's ECG clearly shows an inferior STEMI despite her lack of chest pain. The ST elevation in II III aVF indicates inferior wall involvement typically from right coronary artery occlusion. Clinicians must maintain high suspicion for ACS in high-risk patients even without typical symptoms.

question: A 64-year-old male presents with chest discomfort and shortness of breath that began 90 minutes ago. He describes the discomfort as pressure in his chest that goes straight through to his back between his shoulder blades. He is nauseated and has vomited once. His history includes hypertension atrial fibrillation and he had a stent placed in his right coronary artery three years ago. Medications include metoprolol diltiazem apixaban and aspirin. Vital signs are BP 168/94 HR 78 irregular RR 22 SpO2 93% on room air. You obtain a 12-lead ECG which shows atrial fibrillation with ST depression of 2-3mm in leads V1 V2 and V3 with tall R waves in V1 and V2. There is no ST elevation in any lead. What does this ECG pattern suggest?
option_a: Anterior wall ischemia only
option_b: This pattern suggests posterior wall STEMI which appears as ST depression and tall R waves in V1-V3. Obtain posterior leads V7-V9
option_c: The patient is not having an MI because there is no ST elevation
option_d: Left bundle branch block
rationale: ST depression in V1-V3 with tall R waves is the mirror image of what would be ST elevation and Q waves if you could see the posterior wall directly. This pattern strongly suggests posterior wall STEMI typically from occlusion of the left circumflex or distal right coronary artery. To confirm obtain posterior leads V7 V8 V9 which will show ST elevation of 0.5mm or greater if posterior MI is present. Posterior MIs are frequently missed because standard 12-lead does not directly view the posterior wall. This patient needs the same emergent treatment as any STEMI.

question: You are called to evaluate a 55-year-old male who experienced a syncopal episode at work. He is now conscious and complaining of mild chest discomfort and dizziness. He has no significant medical history and takes no medications. He appears pale and diaphoretic. Vital signs are BP 86/54 HR 44 RR 18 SpO2 96% on room air. You obtain a 12-lead ECG which shows sinus bradycardia at 44 bpm with ST elevation of 4mm in leads II III and aVF. You note ST depression in I and aVL and there is ST elevation of 2mm in V4R on a right-sided ECG. Why is this patient hypotensive and bradycardic and what treatment must you avoid?
option_a: He is in cardiogenic shock and needs immediate dopamine
option_b: The inferior STEMI with right ventricular involvement causes preload-dependent hypotension. Avoid nitroglycerin and diuretics which reduce preload
option_c: He needs immediate transcutaneous pacing for the bradycardia
option_d: The bradycardia is causing the hypotension and atropine will fix everything
rationale: Inferior STEMI frequently involves the right ventricle because the RCA supplies both areas. RV infarction makes cardiac output extremely dependent on adequate preload. The damaged RV cannot compensate for any reduction in filling pressure. Nitroglycerin causes venodilation reducing preload and can cause profound hypotension or cardiovascular collapse in RV infarct. Similarly diuretics reduce preload and are contraindicated. Treatment includes IV fluid boluses to maintain preload and avoiding nitrates and diuretics. The bradycardia results from RCA also supplying the SA and AV nodes.

question: A 48-year-old female presents with intermittent chest pain over the past 2 days that has become constant over the past 3 hours. She describes sharp pain that worsens when she takes a deep breath or lies flat and improves when she sits up and leans forward. She had a viral upper respiratory infection last week. She has no cardiac history and takes only birth control pills. Vital signs are BP 118/72 HR 88 RR 18 SpO2 98% on room air. You obtain a 12-lead ECG which shows diffuse ST elevation in leads I II III aVL aVF and V2-V6 with PR segment depression in multiple leads. This presentation is most consistent with:
option_a: Acute anterior STEMI requiring emergent cardiac catheterization
option_b: Acute pericarditis which mimics STEMI but has diffuse ST elevation and PR depression
option_c: Unstable angina
option_d: Pulmonary embolism
rationale: Acute pericarditis causes inflammation of the pericardial sac and can mimic STEMI on ECG. Key differentiating features include diffuse ST elevation not conforming to a coronary artery distribution PR segment depression which is specific for pericarditis positional and pleuritic chest pain and recent viral illness. Unlike STEMI pericarditis does not benefit from reperfusion therapy and anticoagulation for presumed ACS could cause hemorrhagic pericarditis. Treatment includes NSAIDs and colchicine. The classic finding of PR depression reflects atrial inflammation.

question: A 67-year-old male with a history of prior CABG surgery 8 years ago presents with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to both arms that began 1 hour ago. He is diaphoretic pale and anxious. His medications include aspirin metoprolol lisinopril and atorvastatin. Vital signs are BP 148/92 HR 108 RR 24 SpO2 93% on room air. You obtain a 12-lead ECG which shows ST elevation of 2mm in leads V1-V4 with Q waves in V1-V2. The ST segments have a coved or tombstone appearance. What is the significance of the Q waves and ST segment morphology?
option_a: Q waves indicate this is an old MI not acute so no emergent treatment is needed
option_b: The tombstone ST elevation with new Q waves indicates a large acute MI with myocardial necrosis already occurring and high mortality risk
option_c: Q waves are normal variant in leads V1-V2
option_d: The Q waves indicate bundle branch block
rationale: Tombstone ST elevation describes ST segments that are massively elevated with a distinctive morphology associated with large acute MIs and high mortality. The presence of Q waves indicates that transmural myocardial necrosis has already begun. While Q waves can indicate old MI in this context with ongoing symptoms and tombstone ST elevation they represent early infarct evolution and are an ominous sign. This patient has a large anterior STEMI requiring immediate reperfusion. Time to treatment is critical and mortality increases with delay.

question: A 35-year-old male presents with sharp left-sided chest pain that began suddenly 2 hours ago while playing basketball. He has no medical history and takes no medications. He is tall and thin with long arms and fingers. The pain is constant and does not change with breathing or position. Vital signs are BP 158/62 HR 112 RR 20 SpO2 97% on room air. You note a significant blood pressure difference between arms with the right arm BP 158/62 and left arm BP 118/54. A 12-lead ECG shows sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST changes. What condition must you consider in this patient and what treatment is contraindicated?
option_a: This is a classic STEMI presentation and he needs aspirin and heparin immediately
option_b: The BP differential and body habitus suggest possible aortic dissection. Anticoagulation and thrombolytics are absolutely contraindicated
option_c: He is having a panic attack and needs benzodiazepines
option_d: This is costochondritis and needs only NSAIDs
rationale: This patient has features concerning for aortic dissection including sudden onset tearing or sharp chest pain radiating to the back tall thin body habitus suggesting possible Marfan syndrome and greater than 20 mmHg blood pressure differential between arms. Aortic dissection can mimic MI on ECG if the dissection involves coronary ostia. Giving anticoagulation antiplatelet agents or thrombolytics to a patient with aortic dissection can be fatal by promoting hemorrhage. The wide pulse pressure also suggests aortic regurgitation from dissection. Treatment focuses on BP and heart rate control and emergent surgical consultation.

question: A 29-year-old male presents with palpitations and chest discomfort that began 30 minutes ago while at rest. He reports he has experienced similar episodes before that usually resolve on their own. He has no medical history and takes no medications but admits to heavy energy drink consumption today. He appears anxious but not in acute distress. Vital signs are BP 128/78 HR 188 regular RR 18 SpO2 99% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 188 bpm with no discernible P waves. A 12-lead ECG confirms regular narrow complex tachycardia with ST depression in multiple leads. What is the rhythm and are the ST changes indicative of acute MI?
option_a: This is ventricular tachycardia and he needs immediate cardioversion
option_b: This is SVT and the ST depression is rate-related demand ischemia not acute MI. Treat the SVT and ST changes should resolve
option_c: This is atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
option_d: This is sinus tachycardia from anxiety
rationale: Regular narrow complex tachycardia at this rate without visible P waves is supraventricular tachycardia most likely AVNRT or AVRT. The ST depression seen during SVT is typically rate-related demand ischemia caused by increased myocardial oxygen demand at rapid rates not acute coronary occlusion. These ST changes typically resolve when the rhythm is converted to normal sinus. Treatment should focus on terminating the SVT with vagal maneuvers followed by adenosine. Once rhythm is controlled repeat ECG should show resolution of ST changes in the absence of true coronary disease.

question: A 45-year-old female presents with severe dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain that began suddenly 6 hours ago. She recently returned from a 14-hour international flight two days ago. She has a history of obesity and takes oral contraceptives. She appears anxious and is tachypneic. Her right calf is swollen and tender. Vital signs are BP 108/72 HR 124 RR 28 SpO2 88% on room air. A 12-lead ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 124 with right axis deviation S wave in lead I Q wave in lead III and inverted T wave in lead III. This S1Q3T3 pattern combined with her presentation suggests:
option_a: Acute inferior STEMI
option_b: Acute pulmonary embolism causing right heart strain
option_c: Acute pericarditis
option_d: Hyperkalemia
rationale: This patient has multiple risk factors for pulmonary embolism including recent prolonged immobilization obesity and oral contraceptive use. The clinical presentation of sudden dyspnea pleuritic chest pain tachycardia hypoxia and unilateral leg swelling suggesting DVT is classic for PE. The S1Q3T3 pattern on ECG occurs in only 10-20% of PE cases but when present suggests significant right heart strain from large PE. Other ECG findings in PE include right axis deviation right bundle branch block and anterior T wave inversions. Treatment includes anticoagulation and hemodynamic support with possible thrombolytics for massive PE.

question: You respond to a 34-year-old female at a fitness center who developed sudden onset palpitations and lightheadedness during her spin class. She has a history of occasional palpitations but this is the most severe episode. She takes no medications and is otherwise healthy. She is awake alert and oriented but uncomfortable. Vital signs are BP 92/58 HR 210 regular RR 22 SpO2 96% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 210 bpm. You do not see clear P waves but there appears to be a short RP interval. After applying oxygen and establishing IV access what is your first intervention for this rhythm?
option_a: Synchronized cardioversion at 50-100 joules
option_b: Attempt vagal maneuvers such as modified Valsalva or carotid sinus massage
option_c: Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push
option_d: Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes
rationale: Although this patient has borderline low blood pressure she is awake alert and stable enough to attempt vagal maneuvers first. The modified Valsalva maneuver with leg elevation after strain has shown improved conversion rates over traditional Valsalva. Carotid massage is another option after ensuring no carotid bruits or history of stroke. These maneuvers increase vagal tone slowing AV node conduction and can terminate AVNRT and AVRT. If vagal maneuvers fail proceed to adenosine. Cardioversion is reserved for unstable patients with altered mental status severe hypotension or signs of poor perfusion.

question: Continuing with the previous patient vagal maneuvers were unsuccessful. You prepare to administer adenosine. The IV is in the left hand. What is the proper technique for adenosine administration and what should you warn the patient about?
option_a: Adenosine 6 mg slow IV push over 2 minutes followed by saline flush
option_b: Adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push at the most proximal port followed immediately by 20 mL rapid saline flush. Warn patient she may feel chest tightness or flushing and that her heart may pause briefly
option_c: Adenosine 12 mg rapid IV push as initial dose
option_d: Adenosine 6 mg IV push and wait 10 minutes for effect
rationale: Adenosine has an extremely short half-life of less than 10 seconds due to rapid cellular uptake and metabolism. It must be given as a rapid IV push through a proximal port followed immediately by a 20 mL saline flush using a two-syringe stopcock technique. The arm should be elevated. Common side effects include flushing chest discomfort and a sense of impending doom lasting 30-60 seconds. Brief asystole or bradycardia is expected and therapeutic. Patients should be warned about these effects to reduce anxiety. Initial dose is 6 mg with 12 mg for second and third attempts if needed.

question: You administer adenosine 6 mg rapid IV push to the patient with SVT. The monitor shows the rhythm momentarily slows revealing flutter waves at a rate of approximately 300 per minute before the tachycardia resumes at 150 bpm. What is your revised diagnosis and how does this change management?
option_a: The adenosine worked partially so give another 6 mg dose
option_b: The adenosine revealed underlying atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction. The treatment approach changes because adenosine does not terminate flutter
option_c: This is now ventricular tachycardia and needs cardioversion
option_d: The patient has WPW and adenosine is dangerous
rationale: Adenosine's brief AV nodal blockade can unmask underlying atrial rhythms by transiently slowing ventricular response. In this case flutter waves at 300 bpm with 2:1 conduction producing a ventricular rate of 150 were revealed. Atrial flutter is a macro-reentrant atrial arrhythmia that does not respond to adenosine because the reentrant circuit does not involve the AV node. Treatment options for stable atrial flutter include rate control with beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers or rhythm control with synchronized cardioversion at 50-100 joules if indicated. More adenosine will not help.

question: A 28-year-old male presents with palpitations that began one hour ago while studying for exams. He states he has had several similar episodes over the years that usually resolve spontaneously. He has no other medical history. He appears comfortable. Vital signs are BP 132/84 HR 176 regular RR 16 SpO2 99% on room air. The 12-lead ECG shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 176 bpm with short PR interval and delta waves visible on the baseline sinus beats at the end of the strip where the rhythm spontaneously converts. What is the most likely underlying condition and what medication is contraindicated if he develops atrial fibrillation?
option_a: He has AVNRT and all standard treatments are appropriate
option_b: He has Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with an accessory pathway. AV nodal blocking agents like adenosine diltiazem and digoxin are dangerous if he develops atrial fibrillation
option_c: He has inappropriate sinus tachycardia
option_d: He has multifocal atrial tachycardia
rationale: The short PR interval and delta waves indicate pre-excitation from an accessory pathway diagnostic of Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome. While adenosine is generally safe for orthodromic AVRT in WPW if a patient with WPW develops atrial fibrillation AV nodal blocking agents are extremely dangerous. They can preferentially block the AV node causing all conduction to proceed down the accessory pathway which may conduct at rates over 300 bpm potentially degenerating to ventricular fibrillation. For WPW with atrial fibrillation treatment is procainamide or cardioversion. Avoid adenosine diltiazem verapamil beta-blockers and digoxin.

question: A 68-year-old male presents with palpitations and mild dyspnea that he noticed starting yesterday afternoon. He has a history of hypertension diabetes and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. His medications include lisinopril metformin and aspirin. He appears slightly fatigued but comfortable. Vital signs are BP 148/92 HR 142 irregularly irregular RR 20 SpO2 95% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows an irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves and varying R-R intervals. The QRS complexes are narrow. The 12-lead ECG confirms atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. What is the most appropriate rate control agent for this patient?
option_a: Adenosine 6 mg IV push to convert the rhythm
option_b: Diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg IV over 2 minutes
option_c: Metoprolol 5 mg IV
option_d: Given his heart failure with preserved EF either diltiazem or metoprolol would be appropriate. Avoid diltiazem in heart failure with reduced EF
rationale: Rate control is the primary prehospital goal for stable atrial fibrillation with RVR. In patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction both beta-blockers and calcium channel blockers are acceptable. However in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction HFrEF calcium channel blockers particularly diltiazem and verapamil are contraindicated due to negative inotropic effects that can worsen heart failure. Metoprolol or diltiazem can be used here but if EF were reduced only beta-blockers would be appropriate. Adenosine does not convert atrial fibrillation.

question: A 75-year-old female presents with acute onset of dyspnea palpitations and near-syncope that began 30 minutes ago. She has a history of atrial fibrillation but states she is usually in normal rhythm on her medications which include flecainide and metoprolol. She missed her medications for two days. She appears pale diaphoretic and confused. Vital signs are BP 74/48 HR 168 irregularly irregular RR 28 SpO2 88% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response at 168 bpm with narrow QRS complexes. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
option_a: Diltiazem 20 mg IV for rate control
option_b: Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes
option_c: Synchronized cardioversion starting at 120-200 joules biphasic
option_d: Adenosine 12 mg IV push
rationale: This patient is hemodynamically unstable with hypotension altered mental status and signs of poor perfusion. Unstable patients with tachyarrhythmias require immediate synchronized cardioversion regardless of rhythm duration. Cardioversion for atrial fibrillation uses 120-200 joules biphasic starting at the higher end of the range because AFib is harder to cardiovert than other SVTs. Sedation with midazolam or etomidate should be given if time and blood pressure permit but cardioversion should not be delayed for sedation in severely unstable patients. Post-cardioversion rate control and anticoagulation decisions are made in hospital.

question: A 52-year-old male presents with palpitations and fatigue. He was diagnosed with atrial fibrillation one year ago and is on rate control with diltiazem and anticoagulation with apixaban. He is comfortable appearing with no acute distress. Vital signs are BP 138/86 HR 118 irregularly irregular RR 16 SpO2 98% on room air. He asks you about the risks of his condition. Which of the following is the most serious complication of atrial fibrillation that his anticoagulation is intended to prevent?
option_a: Syncope from rapid heart rates
option_b: Stroke from thrombus formation in the fibrillating atria
option_c: Heart attack from coronary artery spasm
option_d: Cardiomyopathy from chronic rapid rates
rationale: The most serious complication of atrial fibrillation is stroke. During AFib the atria do not contract effectively leading to blood stasis particularly in the left atrial appendage. This promotes thrombus formation. If the patient cardioverts or clot dislodges the thrombus can embolize to the brain causing stroke. The CHA2DS2-VASc score estimates stroke risk and guides anticoagulation decisions. AFib increases stroke risk 5-fold. Anticoagulation with warfarin or direct oral anticoagulants significantly reduces this risk. Rate control also helps prevent tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy from chronic rapid rates.

question: A 62-year-old male with a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy and reduced ejection fraction of 25% presents with palpitations and mild lightheadedness. He has an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator ICD placed 2 years ago. He is awake and conversant but appears anxious. Vital signs are BP 98/68 HR 178 regular RR 20 SpO2 94% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a wide complex tachycardia at 178 bpm that is regular with monomorphic QRS complexes. There appears to be AV dissociation with P waves marching through at a different rate. His ICD has not fired. What is the rhythm and what antiarrhythmic would be appropriate?
option_a: This is SVT with aberrant conduction and adenosine is indicated
option_b: This is ventricular tachycardia. Amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes is appropriate for stable VT
option_c: This is atrial fibrillation with bundle branch block
option_d: This is torsades de pointes requiring magnesium
rationale: A wide complex tachycardia in a patient with structural heart disease is VT until proven otherwise. The presence of AV dissociation with P waves independent of QRS complexes confirms ventricular origin because in VT the ventricles beat independently of atrial activity. His ICD may not have fired because the rate is below the programmed therapy zone or the ICD is sensing the rhythm differently. For hemodynamically stable VT amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes is first-line followed by infusion. Alternative is lidocaine. If the patient becomes unstable synchronized cardioversion is indicated.

question: A 58-year-old female with no known cardiac history presents after a syncopal episode at home. She is now awake but states she feels her heart racing. She takes no medications regularly but admits to drinking 5-6 cups of coffee and taking multiple over-the-counter diet pills today. She appears anxious and mildly diaphoretic. Vital signs are BP 86/54 HR 220 regular RR 24 SpO2 94% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a wide complex tachycardia at 220 bpm that is regular but the QRS morphology appears to vary slightly in axis from beat to beat with a sinusoidal appearance. What is the most likely rhythm and what is the treatment?
option_a: Monomorphic VT treat with amiodarone
option_b: Polymorphic VT degenerating toward torsades de pointes. This patient is unstable and needs immediate defibrillation
option_c: SVT with aberrancy treat with adenosine
option_d: Atrial flutter with aberrant conduction
rationale: The wide complex tachycardia with varying QRS axis and sinusoidal appearance suggests polymorphic VT which can degenerate to ventricular fibrillation. The stimulant use coffee and diet pills may have triggered this through catecholamine excess or QT prolongation from the diet pills. Unlike stable monomorphic VT polymorphic VT is immediately life-threatening and in an unstable patient requires immediate defibrillation not synchronized cardioversion because the varying QRS morphology makes synchronization unreliable. After defibrillation identify and treat underlying cause including electrolyte abnormalities and QT-prolonging substances.

question: A 45-year-old female presents with palpitations and dizziness. She has a history of long QT syndrome and takes nadolol for this condition. She admits she ran out of her medication a week ago. She is awake but lightheaded. Vital signs are BP 94/62 HR 200 RR 20 SpO2 96% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a wide complex tachycardia at approximately 200 bpm with a twisting axis that appears to rotate around the isoelectric line. The QRS complexes appear to point up then gradually rotate to point down then back up again. What is this rhythm and what is the specific treatment?
option_a: Monomorphic VT treat with amiodarone 300 mg IV
option_b: Torsades de pointes treat with magnesium sulfate 2 grams IV and consider overdrive pacing
option_c: Atrial fibrillation with WPW treat with procainamide
option_d: Ventricular fibrillation treat with defibrillation
rationale: Torsades de pointes meaning twisting of the points is a specific form of polymorphic VT that occurs in the setting of prolonged QT interval. The characteristic ECG pattern shows QRS complexes that appear to rotate around the baseline changing from upright to inverted in a sinusoidal pattern. Treatment differs from standard VT because standard antiarrhythmics like amiodarone can further prolong QT and worsen torsades. Magnesium sulfate 2 grams IV is first-line even with normal magnesium levels. If unstable defibrillate. Overdrive pacing at rates of 100-120 shortens QT and can prevent recurrence.

question: A 78-year-old male presents with weakness dizziness and near-syncope that has been worsening over the past day. He has a history of coronary artery disease and takes metoprolol aspirin and atorvastatin. He appears pale and lethargic. Vital signs are BP 84/52 HR 36 regular RR 16 SpO2 94% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a regular bradycardia at 36 bpm. The 12-lead ECG shows sinus rhythm with P waves present but the PR interval is constant at 0.24 seconds and every QRS complex is preceded by a P wave. No P waves are non-conducted. What type of heart block is present and what is the initial treatment?
option_a: First degree AV block which does not require treatment
option_b: This appears to be marked sinus bradycardia with first degree AV block. Initial treatment is atropine 0.5 mg IV
option_c: Third degree AV block requiring transcutaneous pacing
option_d: Second degree Type II requiring transcutaneous pacing
rationale: This patient has sinus bradycardia at 36 bpm with first degree AV block characterized by a prolonged but constant PR interval with all P waves conducting to QRS complexes. First degree block alone does not cause hemodynamic compromise but the extremely slow sinus rate is causing his symptoms. Initial treatment for symptomatic bradycardia is atropine 0.5 mg IV every 3-5 minutes to maximum 3 mg. If atropine fails to increase rate or improve symptoms transcutaneous pacing is indicated. Beta-blocker toxicity should be considered given his medication history and may be contributing to the severe bradycardia.

question: A 82-year-old female presents with syncope. Her family states she suddenly became pale and collapsed but regained consciousness after about 20 seconds. She has a history of hypertension and takes amlodipine only. She is now awake and denies chest pain or dyspnea but feels weak. Vital signs are BP 108/68 HR 38 RR 16 SpO2 97% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a slow regular rhythm at 38 bpm. The 12-lead ECG shows P waves at a rate of 80 that are completely independent of QRS complexes at 38. The QRS complexes are narrow at 0.08 seconds. What is the rhythm and is atropine likely to be effective?
option_a: Sinus bradycardia atropine will be effective
option_b: Third degree complete heart block with junctional escape. Atropine may provide temporary improvement but pacing is likely needed
option_c: Second degree Type I atropine will be effective
option_d: Sinus arrest with junctional escape
rationale: Complete or third degree heart block is characterized by complete AV dissociation where P waves and QRS complexes are completely independent with atrial rate faster than ventricular rate. The narrow QRS suggests a junctional escape rhythm arising from above the bundle branches. Atropine may provide temporary benefit by increasing the rate of the escape pacemaker but is often ineffective especially if block is infranodal. Transcutaneous pacing should be prepared immediately and may be needed. This patient will ultimately require permanent pacemaker placement.

question: A 65-year-old male with a history of anterior MI two weeks ago presents with dizziness and one syncopal episode. He was discharged from the hospital 5 days ago on aspirin clopidogrel metoprolol lisinopril and atorvastatin. Vital signs are BP 92/58 HR 32 RR 18 SpO2 95% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a very slow rhythm at 32 bpm. The 12-lead ECG shows P waves at 78 bpm with no consistent relationship to QRS complexes which occur at 32 bpm with wide QRS duration of 0.16 seconds. Atropine 0.5 mg IV is given with no improvement. What is the significance of the wide QRS and what is your next intervention?
option_a: The wide QRS indicates bundle branch block but has no prognostic significance. Give more atropine
option_b: The wide QRS indicates ventricular escape rhythm from infranodal block which typically does not respond to atropine. Immediate transcutaneous pacing is indicated
option_c: The wide QRS indicates hyperkalemia. Give calcium chloride
option_d: This is accelerated idioventricular rhythm which is benign post-MI
rationale: Complete heart block with wide QRS complex indicates the escape rhythm is arising from below the AV junction in the ventricles suggesting infranodal block. This is particularly common after anterior MI because the LAD supplies the bundle branches. Infranodal block has a worse prognosis than block at the AV node level and ventricular escape rhythms are slower and less reliable. Atropine is typically ineffective because it acts on the AV node not the ventricular escape focus. Immediate transcutaneous pacing is indicated. This patient will likely require permanent pacemaker.

question: A 56-year-old female presents with weakness and dizziness over the past several hours. She has a history of depression and her family found her with empty pill bottles nearby. On exam she is lethargic and confused. Vital signs are BP 78/44 HR 44 wide complex RR 14 SpO2 95% on room air. Blood glucose is 98 mg/dL. The cardiac monitor shows a wide complex bradycardia at 44 bpm. The 12-lead shows QRS duration of 160 ms with a rightward axis. There is a long QTc interval. Given the suspected tricyclic antidepressant overdose what intervention may improve cardiac conduction and blood pressure?
option_a: Atropine 1 mg IV for the bradycardia
option_b: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV to narrow the QRS and improve conduction
option_c: Glucagon 5 mg IV for the hypotension
option_d: Calcium chloride 1 gram IV
rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants cause cardiotoxicity through sodium channel blockade which manifests as QRS widening and can progress to ventricular arrhythmias. The right axis deviation and terminal R wave in aVR are characteristic of TCA toxicity. Sodium bicarbonate is the treatment of choice because it provides sodium to overcome channel blockade and alkalinization reduces drug binding to sodium channels. The goal is to narrow QRS to less than 100 ms and maintain pH 7.50-7.55. Atropine may help with anticholinergic effects but does not address sodium channel blockade. Glucagon is for beta-blocker overdose.

question: You are treating a 74-year-old male with symptomatic third degree heart block and hypotension. Atropine was ineffective and you have initiated transcutaneous pacing. The pacemaker is set at 70 ppm and you are increasing the milliamps. At 60 mA you see pacer spikes on the monitor but no QRS complexes following them. At 80 mA you see pacer spikes followed by wide QRS complexes. How do you confirm mechanical capture and what should you set the final milliamp output?
option_a: Electrical capture is confirmed by QRS following each spike. Set output at 80 mA
option_b: Mechanical capture is confirmed by a pulse with each paced beat. Set output at approximately 90 mA which is 10% above threshold
option_c: Capture is confirmed by patient comfort. Set output at the lowest tolerable level
option_d: Capture is confirmed by blood pressure improvement alone
rationale: Electrical capture occurs when each pacer spike produces a QRS complex but this does not guarantee effective mechanical contraction of the heart. Mechanical capture must be confirmed by assessing for a pulse with each paced beat either by palpation or arterial waveform. Once capture threshold is determined at 80 mA in this case set output 10% above threshold typically adding 5-10 mA to ensure consistent capture. The patient will need sedation for comfort as transcutaneous pacing is painful. Prepare for transvenous pacing if transport is prolonged.

question: You respond to a 59-year-old male who collapsed at a gym. Bystanders report he was on the treadmill when he suddenly grabbed his chest and fell. A bystander immediately started CPR and an AED was applied which delivered one shock before your arrival approximately 6 minutes after collapse. Upon your arrival CPR is ongoing and the patient is unresponsive with no pulse. The cardiac monitor shows coarse ventricular fibrillation. What is the correct sequence of interventions for this patient in VF arrest?
option_a: Intubate first then establish IV then shock then CPR
option_b: Continue CPR while charging defibrillator then clear and shock at maximum joules then immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes before rhythm check
option_c: Check pulse for 30 seconds then shock if still in VF then start CPR
option_d: Administer epinephrine first then shock then CPR
rationale: High-quality CPR and early defibrillation are the priorities in VF arrest. Upon confirming VF continue CPR while charging the defibrillator. Deliver shock at maximum joules for biphasic defibrillator typically 200J then immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes without rhythm or pulse check. Interruptions in CPR should be minimized. After 2 minutes check rhythm. If VF continues shock again and give epinephrine 1 mg IV. Continue cycles of 2 minutes CPR rhythm check shock with epinephrine every 3-5 minutes and amiodarone after second shock.

question: Continuing with the gym arrest patient after your first shock the rhythm remains VF. You resume CPR and your partner establishes IV access. After the second shock VF persists. Per ACLS protocol what medications should be administered at this point?
option_a: Amiodarone 300 mg only
option_b: Epinephrine 1 mg IV and amiodarone 300 mg IV
option_c: Atropine 1 mg IV and lidocaine 100 mg IV
option_d: Vasopressin 40 units IV and magnesium 2 grams IV
rationale: After the second shock for persistent VF epinephrine 1 mg IV should be given to maximize coronary and cerebral perfusion during CPR. Epinephrine is repeated every 3-5 minutes. Amiodarone 300 mg IV push is the first-line antiarrhythmic for refractory VF given after the second shock with a second dose of 150 mg if needed. Lidocaine is an alternative if amiodarone unavailable. Atropine is no longer recommended for cardiac arrest. Vasopressin was removed from ACLS algorithm. Continue CPR for 2 minutes after medications before next rhythm check.

question: After 15 minutes of resuscitation the gym arrest patient remains in refractory VF despite 5 shocks epinephrine 1 mg x 4 doses and amiodarone 300 mg plus 150 mg. CPR quality has been excellent with end-tidal CO2 consistently above 20 mmHg. What additional interventions might be considered for refractory VF and what does the EtCO2 tell you?
option_a: Terminate resuscitation as VF is not survivable after 15 minutes
option_b: Consider double sequential defibrillation changing pad position lidocaine esmolol. The EtCO2 above 20 indicates adequate CPR quality and continued viability
option_c: Give sodium bicarbonate 100 mEq for presumed acidosis
option_d: Switch to asystole protocol since VF is refractory
rationale: Refractory VF may respond to alternative strategies. Double sequential defibrillation using two defibrillators delivers shocks in rapid succession and may terminate VF when standard shocks fail. Changing defibrillator pad position to AP may improve vector. Lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg can be tried if amiodarone failed. Esmolol for sympathetic storm is emerging evidence. EtCO2 above 20 mmHg indicates adequate cardiac output during CPR and suggests the patient remains potentially viable. EtCO2 below 10 mmHg during quality CPR suggests poor prognosis.

question: You respond to a 67-year-old female who collapsed at home. Her husband reports she had been complaining of severe abdominal pain and vomited blood this morning before collapsing. She is unresponsive and pulseless. CPR is initiated. The cardiac monitor shows an organized rhythm with narrow QRS complexes at a rate of 72 bpm but there is no pulse. What is this rhythm and what is the most likely underlying cause?
option_a: Ventricular fibrillation from cardiac cause
option_b: PEA likely from hypovolemia due to massive GI hemorrhage
option_c: Asystole
option_d: Sinus rhythm with pulse that you cannot palpate
rationale: Pulseless electrical activity PEA is an organized cardiac rhythm without detectable pulse. The narrow complex organized rhythm at a reasonable rate suggests the heart is trying to function but is unable to generate effective output. Given the history of hematemesis and abdominal pain massive hypovolemia from GI hemorrhage is the most likely cause. PEA treatment focuses on identifying and treating reversible causes using the Hs and Ts mnemonic. For hypovolemia aggressive IV fluid resuscitation and transport for blood transfusion and surgical intervention are needed along with high-quality CPR and epinephrine every 3-5 minutes.

question: You are resuscitating a 55-year-old male found in cardiac arrest at home. His wife states he has kidney disease and missed dialysis twice this week. CPR is in progress. The cardiac monitor shows a wide complex bradycardic rhythm at 28 bpm without pulse. The QRS complexes are very wide at 200 ms and have a sine wave appearance. P waves are absent. What electrolyte abnormality is most likely and what is the immediate treatment?
option_a: Hyponatremia give normal saline bolus
option_b: Severe hyperkalemia give calcium chloride 1 gram IV immediately
option_c: Hypocalcemia give calcium gluconate
option_d: Hypomagnesemia give magnesium sulfate
rationale: The wide complex bradycardia with sine wave pattern and absent P waves in a dialysis patient is classic for severe hyperkalemia. Elevated potassium causes progressive ECG changes starting with peaked T waves then PR prolongation then P wave loss then QRS widening and finally sine wave pattern preceding VF and asystole. Calcium chloride 1 gram IV or calcium gluconate 3 grams IV should be given immediately to stabilize the cardiac membrane. This does not lower potassium but protects against lethal arrhythmias. Follow with sodium bicarbonate insulin with glucose and albuterol to shift potassium intracellularly.

question: A 42-year-old male is found in cardiac arrest at a construction site after a coworker reports he was struck in the chest by a piece of pipe. CPR is in progress. The cardiac monitor shows sinus rhythm at 98 bpm but there is no pulse. His neck veins are distended and heart sounds are muffled. The trachea is midline and breath sounds are equal bilaterally. What is the most likely cause of this PEA arrest and what intervention is needed?
option_a: Tension pneumothorax perform needle decompression
option_b: Cardiac tamponade from blunt cardiac injury. Pericardiocentesis or thoracotomy is needed
option_c: Commotio cordis defibrillate immediately
option_d: Massive hemothorax perform chest tube
rationale: This patient has Beck triad of hypotension distended neck veins and muffled heart sounds indicating cardiac tamponade. In the setting of chest trauma this likely results from blunt cardiac injury with bleeding into the pericardial sac. The blood compresses the heart preventing filling and causing PEA. Unlike tension pneumothorax trachea is midline and breath sounds are equal. Treatment requires pericardiocentesis to remove blood from pericardial sac or emergency thoracotomy. Needle pericardiocentesis can be attempted using subxiphoid approach but traumatic tamponade often has clotted blood requiring surgical drainage.

question: You respond to a 78-year-old female in a nursing home with reported cardiac arrest. Staff state she has metastatic cancer and was recently transitioned to comfort care but does not have a DNR on file. She is unresponsive and pulseless. The cardiac monitor shows a flat line. Before declaring asystole what must you verify?
option_a: Check blood glucose
option_b: Confirm asystole in at least two leads and verify connections gain and lead selection
option_c: Check pupil response
option_d: Get family confirmation of DNR status
rationale: Before accepting a flat line as true asystole you must rule out technical issues. Confirm asystole in at least two different leads as fine VF can appear as flat line in one lead. Check that leads are properly connected gain is turned up and monitor is functioning. If available turn up gain to maximum to look for fine VF. True asystole is confirmed when flat line persists in multiple leads with proper connections. Asystole has very poor prognosis and is not a shockable rhythm. Treatment includes CPR epinephrine and addressing reversible causes but overall survival is less than 2%.

question: After 12 minutes of resuscitation your VF arrest patient has ROSC. He is now unconscious with BP 84/52 HR 92 RR 8 agonal and SpO2 88% on BVM. The 12-lead ECG shows ST elevation in V1-V4 consistent with his presenting anterior STEMI. What are the immediate post-ROSC management priorities?
option_a: Transport to the nearest hospital for observation
option_b: Optimize ventilation and oxygenation treat hypotension transport to PCI-capable center consider targeted temperature management
option_c: Hyperventilate to prevent cerebral edema
option_d: Allow permissive hypotension to prevent pulmonary edema
rationale: Post-ROSC care focuses on preventing rearrest and secondary brain injury. Optimize oxygenation targeting SpO2 94-98% and avoid hyperventilation which causes cerebral vasoconstriction. Treat hypotension with fluids and vasopressors targeting SBP above 90 and MAP above 65 to maintain cerebral perfusion. For STEMI patients emergent PCI is indicated regardless of neurological status as reperfusion improves both cardiac and neurological outcomes. Targeted temperature management at 32-36 degrees C improves neurological outcomes in comatose post-arrest patients. Begin transport to PCI-capable center with TTM capability.

question: Your post-arrest STEMI patient has ROSC and you have secured the airway with endotracheal intubation. Blood pressure remains 78/48 despite 1 liter normal saline bolus. The cardiac monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 112 without ectopy. SpO2 is 96% on ventilator. What vasopressor would be most appropriate and what is the target blood pressure?
option_a: Dopamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min targeting SBP above 90
option_b: Norepinephrine or epinephrine infusion targeting SBP above 90 and MAP above 65
option_c: Phenylephrine 100-200 mcg boluses
option_d: Vasopressin 0.04 units/min
rationale: Post-arrest hypotension unresponsive to fluids requires vasopressor support. Norepinephrine or epinephrine infusions are first-line for post-arrest shock. Target SBP above 90 mmHg and MAP above 65 mmHg to maintain coronary and cerebral perfusion. Dopamine is an alternative but has higher arrhythmia risk. Phenylephrine is a pure vasoconstrictor without inotropic effects and is less ideal when myocardial dysfunction is present. Start infusion at low dose and titrate to target BP. Prepare for cath lab as reperfusion may improve hemodynamics.

question: A 72-year-old male presents with severe dyspnea that woke him from sleep. He has a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction of 30% hypertension and diabetes. His medications include furosemide carvedilol lisinopril and metformin. He is sitting bolt upright and cannot lie back. He has pink frothy sputum. On exam he has jugular venous distension crackles throughout both lung fields and 2+ lower extremity edema. Vital signs are BP 198/112 HR 118 RR 32 SpO2 82% on room air. What is the pathophysiology and what are the treatment priorities?
option_a: He is volume overloaded and needs aggressive diuresis with furosemide 80 mg IV
option_b: He has acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema from LV failure. Treatment priorities are reduce preload with nitroglycerin support oxygenation with CPAP and diuretics
option_c: He is in hypertensive crisis and needs labetalol to lower BP
option_d: He has pneumonia and needs antibiotics
rationale: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema occurs when the failing left ventricle cannot handle venous return causing back-pressure into the pulmonary circulation and fluid transudation into alveoli. The hypertension increases afterload worsening cardiac output. Treatment focuses on reducing preload with nitroglycerin which also reduces afterload supporting oxygenation with CPAP or BiPAP which reduces work of breathing and can improve cardiac function and diuretics to remove excess fluid. Nitro can be given sublingually or IV starting at 0.4 mg SL every 5 minutes or 10-20 mcg/min IV titrated.

question: Continuing with the CHF patient you have applied CPAP at 10 cmH2O and administered nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual times two. His SpO2 has improved to 92% but his blood pressure has dropped to 88/54 and he is becoming more lethargic. What should you do now?
option_a: Continue nitroglycerin as BP is still adequate
option_b: Stop nitroglycerin immediately as he is now hypotensive and consider this may be cardiogenic shock requiring different management
option_c: Increase CPAP to 15 cmH2O
option_d: Give furosemide 80 mg IV push
rationale: This patient has transitioned from hypertensive pulmonary edema which responds to preload reduction to cardiogenic shock characterized by hypotension and signs of hypoperfusion. In cardiogenic shock the heart cannot generate adequate output and preload reduction with nitrates worsens hypotension. Treatment shifts from vasodilators to inotropic support. Dobutamine or dopamine may be needed to support cardiac output. Consider whether this represents acute coronary syndrome causing pump failure. Stop CPAP if causing hemodynamic compromise. This patient needs emergent transport to facility with cardiac care capabilities.

question: A 68-year-old male presents with progressive dyspnea and bilateral leg swelling over the past 3 weeks. He has a history of COPD and was recently diagnosed with lung cancer. He has no known cardiac history. He is dyspneic at rest but comfortable sitting up. Vital signs are BP 142/88 HR 102 RR 24 SpO2 90% on 4L NC. On exam he has markedly elevated JVP bilateral lower extremity pitting edema to the thighs and a right pleural effusion. Heart sounds are distant but regular without murmurs. His liver is palpable and tender. The ECG shows low voltage and electrical alternans. What is the most likely diagnosis?
option_a: Left heart failure from occult coronary disease
option_b: Right heart failure possibly from cardiac tamponade related to malignancy. Electrical alternans is concerning
option_c: Cor pulmonale from COPD
option_d: Bilateral DVT causing leg swelling
rationale: This patient has signs of right heart failure with elevated JVP edema and hepatic congestion but without pulmonary edema suggesting the left heart is not primarily involved. In a patient with malignancy pericardial effusion with tamponade must be considered. Electrical alternans is a swinging heart pattern where QRS amplitude varies beat to beat and is highly suggestive of large pericardial effusion. Malignancy is a common cause of hemorrhagic pericardial effusion. This patient needs echocardiography and possibly pericardiocentesis. The distant heart sounds also suggest fluid around the heart.

question: A 64-year-old female presents with crushing chest pain and severe dyspnea. She is pale diaphoretic and extremely anxious. She has a history of hypertension and diabetes. Vital signs are BP 72/48 HR 126 RR 32 SpO2 84% on room air. She has crackles in both lung fields and cool mottled extremities. The 12-lead shows ST elevation in V1-V6 I and aVL consistent with large anterolateral STEMI. She is alert but confused. What type of shock is this patient in and what are the treatment priorities?
option_a: Septic shock treat with fluids and antibiotics
option_b: Cardiogenic shock from massive MI. Treatment includes cautious fluids avoiding overload inotropic support emergent reperfusion and possibly mechanical support
option_c: Hypovolemic shock from GI bleeding give large fluid bolus
option_d: Anaphylactic shock give epinephrine
rationale: This patient has cardiogenic shock complicating a large anterior STEMI. Cardiogenic shock occurs when the heart cannot pump adequately to meet tissue demands typically when more than 40% of myocardium is damaged. Signs include hypotension tachycardia pulmonary congestion and end-organ hypoperfusion. Unlike hypovolemic shock large fluid boluses worsen pulmonary edema. Treatment includes cautious fluids vasopressors and inotropes like norepinephrine and dobutamine and emergent reperfusion via PCI which is the most important intervention. Mechanical support with IABP or Impella may be needed.

question: You are transporting the cardiogenic shock patient from the previous question. You have established IV access and given 250 mL fluid bolus with minimal improvement. BP remains 74/50. Medical control orders you to start a vasopressor infusion. What is the most appropriate vasopressor choice for cardiogenic shock?
option_a: Phenylephrine 100-200 mcg/min
option_b: Norepinephrine starting at 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP above 65
option_c: Dopamine at 20 mcg/kg/min
option_d: Vasopressin 0.04 units/min
rationale: Current evidence supports norepinephrine as the first-line vasopressor for cardiogenic shock. The SOAP II trial showed dopamine was associated with more arrhythmias and mortality in cardiogenic shock subgroup. Norepinephrine provides both vasoconstriction and some inotropic effect. Starting dose is 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP above 65 mmHg. Dobutamine can be added if additional inotropy is needed once adequate perfusion pressure is achieved. Phenylephrine is a pure vasoconstrictor and may worsen cardiac output by increasing afterload. The definitive treatment remains emergent reperfusion.

question: A 25-year-old male presents after a motorcycle crash. He was not wearing a helmet and has obvious head trauma. He also has a deformed left femur and abdominal bruising. He is confused and combative. Vital signs are BP 78/50 HR 138 RR 28 SpO2 94% on room air. His skin is pale cool and diaphoretic. Capillary refill is 5 seconds. What class of hemorrhagic shock is this patient in and what are the fluid resuscitation priorities?
option_a: Class I shock with 15% blood loss give 500 mL NS
option_b: Class III or IV shock with 30-40% or greater blood loss. Priorities are hemorrhage control permissive hypotension and blood products not crystalloid
option_c: Class II shock with 15-30% blood loss give 2L crystalloid
option_d: This is neurogenic shock from head trauma not hypovolemic
rationale: This patient has signs of severe hemorrhagic shock including marked hypotension severe tachycardia altered mental status and poor perfusion indicating Class III or IV hemorrhage with greater than 30% blood volume loss. Modern trauma resuscitation emphasizes damage control resuscitation with limited crystalloid to avoid dilutional coagulopathy permissive hypotension maintaining SBP 80-90 in penetrating trauma and early blood products in 1:1:1 ratio of PRBC plasma and platelets. Hemorrhage control takes priority. Large crystalloid volumes are associated with worse outcomes in hemorrhagic shock.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with fever and confusion. His wife reports he has been sick for 3 days with cough and fever and today became very confused. He has no significant medical history. Vital signs are BP 72/38 HR 128 RR 26 SpO2 91% on room air Temp 103.4F. His skin is warm and flushed initially but his extremities are becoming mottled. Capillary refill is 4 seconds. What type of shock is this and what is the initial fluid resuscitation target?
option_a: Cardiogenic shock from myocarditis
option_b: Septic shock from pneumonia. Initial fluid resuscitation is 30 mL/kg crystalloid within the first hour
option_c: Anaphylactic shock give epinephrine
option_d: Neurogenic shock from fever affecting the brain
rationale: This patient has septic shock characterized by suspected infection with life-threatening organ dysfunction manifested by hypotension altered mental status and hypoperfusion. Early septic shock may have warm flushed skin from vasodilation but progresses to cold mottled skin as compensatory mechanisms fail. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign recommends 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus within the first hour. If hypotension persists after adequate fluid resuscitation vasopressors norepinephrine first-line should be started. Early antibiotics within one hour and source control are critical. Lactate measurement helps guide resuscitation.

question: A 32-year-old female with a known severe peanut allergy accidentally ate something containing peanuts at a restaurant. Within 5 minutes she developed hives lip swelling difficulty breathing and feels faint. She used her EpiPen before EMS arrival. On exam she has diffuse urticaria angioedema of face and lips inspiratory stridor and expiratory wheezing. Vital signs are BP 78/42 HR 142 RR 32 SpO2 86% on room air. Despite the EpiPen she remains in shock. What is the next medication intervention?
option_a: Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV as it works better than epinephrine
option_b: Repeat epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM as the first dose was insufficient
option_c: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV to reduce inflammation
option_d: Albuterol nebulizer for the wheezing
rationale: Anaphylactic shock causes distributive shock through massive histamine-mediated vasodilation and capillary leak. Epinephrine is the only life-saving medication and should be repeated every 5-15 minutes if symptoms persist. The IM dose is 0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration. This patient remains in shock with airway compromise requiring repeat epinephrine. IV epinephrine infusion may be needed for refractory cases. Adjunct medications include H1 blockers diphenhydramine H2 blockers famotidine corticosteroids and bronchodilators but these should never delay epinephrine. Aggressive IV fluids are also needed for the hypotension.

question: A 62-year-old female post-operative day 5 from hip replacement surgery presents with sudden onset severe dyspnea and chest pain. She has been mostly bedbound since surgery. She is extremely anxious and dyspneic. Vital signs are BP 82/58 HR 132 RR 34 SpO2 82% on room air. Her neck veins are distended. Lung sounds are clear. The 12-lead shows sinus tachycardia with right axis deviation S1Q3T3 pattern and T wave inversions in V1-V4. What is the most likely diagnosis and what treatment should be considered?
option_a: Acute anterior STEMI needs emergent PCI
option_b: Massive pulmonary embolism causing obstructive shock. Consider thrombolytics if available
option_c: Tension pneumothorax needs needle decompression
option_d: Cardiac tamponade needs pericardiocentesis
rationale: This patient has classic risk factors and presentation for massive pulmonary embolism causing obstructive shock. Recent surgery and immobilization are major risk factors. The combination of hypoxia hypotension clear lungs elevated JVD and right heart strain on ECG is characteristic. In massive PE with shock thrombolytics may be life-saving despite bleeding risks. tPA 100 mg IV over 2 hours or accelerated regimens can be used. If thrombolytics are not available focus on supportive care with fluids cautious as RV is preload-sensitive vasopressors and oxygenation. Surgical embolectomy or catheter-based intervention are alternatives.

question: A 22-year-old male presents after being stabbed in the left chest. He is anxious and dyspneic. On exam he has a small stab wound at the left 5th intercostal space near the sternum. His neck veins are markedly distended. Heart sounds are muffled and distant. Breath sounds are equal bilaterally. Vital signs are BP 68/44 HR 138 RR 28 SpO2 96% on room air. Trachea is midline. What is causing this patient's shock and what is the definitive treatment?
option_a: Tension pneumothorax perform bilateral needle decompression
option_b: Cardiac tamponade from penetrating cardiac injury. Definitive treatment is surgical thoracotomy but pericardiocentesis may temporize
option_c: Massive hemothorax perform chest tube thoracostomy
option_d: Hypovolemic shock from blood loss give massive transfusion
rationale: This patient has penetrating cardiac injury with cardiac tamponade characterized by Beck triad of hypotension distended neck veins and muffled heart sounds. The wound location near the sternum at the 5th ICS is in the cardiac box. Unlike tension pneumothorax breath sounds are equal and trachea is midline. Tamponade causes obstructive shock as blood in the pericardium prevents ventricular filling. Definitive treatment is surgical thoracotomy with pericardiotomy and cardiac repair. Pericardiocentesis removing as little as 20-30 mL may temporarily improve hemodynamics but traumatic tamponade often involves clotted blood requiring surgery.

question: A 58-year-old male presents with severe headache blurred vision and chest discomfort. He has a history of hypertension but admits to not taking his medications for several months. On exam he is alert but anxious and diaphoretic. He has papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Vital signs are BP 242/138 HR 98 RR 18 SpO2 98% on room air. The 12-lead shows LVH with strain pattern but no acute ST changes. What is this patient's condition and how should blood pressure be managed?
option_a: He has hypertensive urgency and can be managed with oral medications and outpatient follow-up
option_b: He has hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage manifested by encephalopathy and retinopathy. IV antihypertensives should reduce MAP by 20-25% in the first hour
option_c: He needs immediate BP reduction to normal with IV labetalol
option_d: This is not a true emergency and he should see his doctor tomorrow
rationale: Hypertensive emergency is defined as severely elevated BP with acute end-organ damage. This patient has hypertensive encephalopathy with headache visual changes and papilledema. Other end-organ damage includes ACS stroke aortic dissection and acute kidney injury. Management requires IV antihypertensives but excessive or rapid reduction can cause stroke or MI from hypoperfusion. Target is 20-25% reduction in MAP in the first hour then gradual reduction over 24-48 hours. Labetalol or nicardipine IV are commonly used. Hypertensive urgency is severe BP without end-organ damage and can be managed less urgently.

question: A 72-year-old male presents with sudden onset of the worst headache of his life followed by vomiting and confusion. He has a history of hypertension. On exam he is somnolent with a stiff neck and right-sided weakness. Vital signs are BP 228/124 HR 68 RR 18 SpO2 97% on room air. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the blood pressure target?
option_a: Hypertensive encephalopathy reduce BP by 25% in first hour
option_b: Likely hemorrhagic stroke from hypertension. Aggressively reduce SBP to less than 140 mmHg if intracerebral hemorrhage is confirmed
option_c: Ischemic stroke maintain permissive hypertension up to 220/120
option_d: Meningitis do not treat blood pressure
rationale: Thunderclap headache with focal neurological deficits and severe hypertension suggests hemorrhagic stroke likely intracerebral hemorrhage or subarachnoid hemorrhage. In intracerebral hemorrhage elevated BP promotes hematoma expansion and worsens outcomes. Current guidelines from AHA/ASA recommend SBP reduction to less than 140 mmHg for ICH patients presenting with SBP 150-220 mmHg. This is more aggressive than ischemic stroke or hypertensive encephalopathy targets. IV nicardipine or labetalol are preferred agents. CT scan is needed to confirm hemorrhage versus ischemia before determining final BP target.

question: A 68-year-old female is being transported 3 days after an anterior STEMI that was treated with PCI. She suddenly develops severe dyspnea and a new loud holosystolic murmur heard best at the left lower sternal border. Her vital signs deteriorate to BP 74/58 HR 128 RR 32 SpO2 84% on 15L NRB. What complication has likely occurred and what is the treatment?
option_a: Papillary muscle rupture causing acute mitral regurgitation
option_b: Ventricular septal rupture causing acute VSD with left-to-right shunt. Treatment is supportive with vasodilators and inotropes en route to emergent surgical repair
option_c: Acute stent thrombosis needs emergent re-catheterization
option_d: Free wall rupture and tamponade needs pericardiocentesis
rationale: Ventricular septal rupture is a mechanical complication of MI occurring typically 3-5 days post-MI when necrotic tissue is weakest. It causes acute left-to-right shunt with right heart volume overload and pulmonary overcirculation leading to cardiogenic shock. The new holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border with thrill is characteristic. This is distinguished from papillary muscle rupture which causes mitral regurgitation with murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Treatment is emergent surgical repair. Medical management with vasodilators to reduce afterload and inotropes is temporizing. IABP may help. Mortality without surgery exceeds 90%.

question: A 71-year-old male who had an inferior STEMI 2 days ago suddenly loses consciousness. The cardiac monitor shows ventricular fibrillation. He is defibrillated successfully and converted to sinus rhythm. He is now awake and hemodynamically stable with BP 118/72. Laboratory results show potassium of 2.9 mEq/L and magnesium of 1.4 mg/dL. What is the likely etiology of his VF arrest and what treatment will help prevent recurrence?
option_a: His stent has thrombosed and he needs emergent re-catheterization
option_b: Post-MI VF triggered by hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia. Aggressive electrolyte replacement will reduce arrhythmia risk
option_c: This is expected post-MI and no specific treatment will help
option_d: He needs an ICD implanted immediately
rationale: Electrolyte abnormalities particularly hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia significantly increase arrhythmia risk in the post-MI setting. Both abnormalities affect cardiac repolarization and lower the threshold for ventricular arrhythmias. Aggressive IV replacement is indicated with potassium targeting level above 4.0 mEq/L and magnesium targeting above 2.0 mg/dL. Diuretics commonly used in heart failure can worsen these abnormalities and levels should be monitored closely. While some post-MI arrhythmias are unavoidable optimizing electrolytes is a modifiable risk factor.

question: A 78-year-old male presents after a syncopal episode while walking to his mailbox. He had no warning and woke up on the ground unsure of how long he was unconscious. He has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide. He denies chest pain or palpitations. Vital signs are BP 148/82 HR 64 RR 16 SpO2 98% on room air. The 12-lead ECG shows normal sinus rhythm with a QRS of 132 ms showing right bundle branch block pattern and left anterior fascicular block. This bifascicular block pattern in a patient with syncope suggests:
option_a: Vasovagal syncope which is benign
option_b: High risk for progressing to complete heart block causing cardiac syncope. He needs monitoring and likely pacemaker evaluation
option_c: Orthostatic hypotension from his medications
option_d: Seizure rather than syncope
rationale: Syncope in a patient with bifascicular block RBBB plus LAFB or LPFB is concerning for intermittent complete heart block causing Stokes-Adams attacks. The conduction system disease evident on ECG indicates one fascicle is functioning and if it fails transiently complete block occurs. These patients are at high risk for sudden death from prolonged asystole. Ambulatory monitoring or electrophysiology study can evaluate for infra-Hisian block. Many will require permanent pacemaker. This is not a benign cause of syncope and requires urgent cardiology evaluation.

question: A 66-year-old male with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator ICD presents with multiple shocks from his device over the past hour. He estimates he has received 8-10 shocks. He is awake and anxious. The cardiac monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 115 bpm with occasional PVCs. Vital signs are BP 138/82 HR 115 RR 20 SpO2 97% on room air. His ICD continues to fire while you are assessing him during what appears to be sinus rhythm. What is the most likely problem and what is the intervention?
option_a: The ICD is appropriately treating VT and no intervention is needed
option_b: The ICD is delivering inappropriate shocks likely due to lead malfunction or oversensing. A magnet placed over the device will inhibit shock therapy
option_c: The patient is in VF and the shocks are appropriate
option_d: The ICD battery is depleted and needs replacement
rationale: Inappropriate ICD shocks can occur from lead fracture sensing artifacts electromagnetic interference oversensing of T waves or SVT being misinterpreted as VT. Multiple shocks for what appears to be sinus rhythm suggests inappropriate therapy. This causes significant patient distress and can be harmful. Placing a magnet over the ICD pulse generator will temporarily inhibit tachyarrhythmia detection and shock delivery. This does not affect pacing function. The magnet should remain in place during transport. The patient needs urgent device interrogation to identify and correct the problem.
